item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my god I got it

This is the 100 Stories Ghost from Japanese stories. It’s where people gather round with 100 candles, and each person takes turn telling ghost stories. After each story a candle is blown out, and the next person tells a new story. When all 100 candles are blown out, this ghost will appear.

The entity was contained because only 93 stories were told before the Foundation managed to contain it. It managed to break free because it tricked us into reading 7 stories it made by messing with his own description.

Damn amazing Entry I must say. Take my +1

Just wanted to give another shoutout to VolgunStrife, Modern_Erasmus, Woedenaz, and TheeSherm for their major contributions towards this project.

I'm happy to see something else I've had a hand in break this milestone… maybe I should do something special, like spoil absolutely everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's well written, and it intruiged me somewhat, though not enough that I'll be going out of my way to sherlock holmes the mystery myself. Because of this, I couldn't possibly downvote this article.

However, this didn't make me feel anything resembling horror, eeriness, or creepiness. Really, it didn't get any emotions out of me beyond that sort of curiosity mentioned above. Reading through this discussion a little has showed me that, as usual when it comes to media, I am generally not in the consensus regarding this, maybe I'm just some soulless husk who never feels anything, but nonetheless I don't feel compelled to upvote this article.

So putting these viewpoints together, I reach my decision of a no-vote.

item.title: Re: SCP-5999 Ending Explained (Mostly)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: Nope, it really is as abusive as I thought it wasn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cannot believe I missed that aspect in my own analysis. The Yakasani no Magatama, Yata no Kagami, and Kusanagi no Tsurugi.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm coming in late to try to piece this together, without. Things I noticed on my own, without having to check the discussion, which has expanded as I've written it and went back to check things, so I'm trying to keep in page order:

My findings
Hide these ramblings!

On 7 Candles, Procedure Sevenfold is interesting… as the name, given the other Biblical and Tanakhic references scattered in here, seems to be a reference to Cain's Mark of Protection and the Sevenfold Vengeance promised to those who harmed him despite it… but the reference seems oddly out-of-place.

On 6 Candles, it's… strongly implied that Jacqueline Holcroft was pregnant, and she had her fetus and/or uterus removed, only reinforced in some later pages, but it's hard to have any idea what to make of this.

The exorcism at 4 Candles… doesn't seem to quite make sense to me? At times, it feels almost like he's trying to exorcise Grace rather than whatever demon, but there's other pieces that don't seem to make sense in that case. Doctor Macneil's choice of Deuteronomy 4:4 is also… interesting. Not only is it originally from the Torah — I'm not familiar with traditional exorcism, but I'd think they'd want stuff that more directly pertains to Christianity — but it… doesn't seem like a terribly relevant choice for an exorcism. It's the Second of Moses' speeches in Deutoronomy, and the excerpt specifically starts with G-d's admonition that those who remain faithful will be protected. (The Christian interpretation is that this section is a command of obedience. I… don't know any of the prevailing Judaic interpretations, but blind obedience without reason or debate is generally not considered a virtue in Judaism, so I imagine it's somewhat different)

There's a few interesting patterns for the different roles in 3:

Profile Alpha: Composed almost solely of children among the ones we have clear ages for. The absolute oldest we have a concrete age for is 18. It's intriguing that it seems to be associated with fire and heat, but *ALSO* with the manifestation of anomalous additional organs. I feel like there's a reference here I either don't know or isn't quite pinging for me at the moment. Tied with Entity A for sure, and seemingly with The Labyrinth, the Prodigy, and the Jewel.

Profile Beta: Except for Cindel and Mandy Shields, who are part of the strange, anomalous-seeming cursive script and thus likely not part of the "unmodified" text of the entry, these seem to all be adults. There's strong suggestions that they're sexual predators — particularly of children. (Ray Gonzales is explicitly established to have had a 6-year-old child in his basement, and one is a priest, which… well, there's certain cultural views there, which exist for the good reason of such predators seeking out positions of power that allow them unsupervised access to children. Meanwhile, one of the D-class testers is identified as a sexual offender, but the kind is not specified.) Tied with Entity B, and seemingly the Crypt, the Mirror, and the Priest. (Which is another interesting factor to one of Profile Beta being a priest)

Profile Omega: This is where things get interesting. With the exception of Sarah Whitaker, these are normally not people who played the game, but who have close ties to those who did. More of them than not also somehow tried to stop people from playing. And except for Sarah Whitaker, whose circumstances are… mysterious, and those killed on the 9th of December, all are killed in ways that could be broadly considered "self-inflicted," whether accidents or suicide, and all except Sarah Whitaker are either hanged or have their cervical vertebrae (those of the neck) damaged — the same vertebrae a hanging is supposed to fracture. Given that, the appearance of the associated Entity-C, and the fact that the Hedge Mage is associated with the Sword and more importantly King, this whole profile fairly *stinks* of being associated in some way with the Hanged King and Alagadda.

On 2 Candles, Cindy *has* to be the Cindel referenced in the previous part. The weird version of "Twinkle Twinkle Little Star" that's referenced repeatedly and has its lyrics provided for the first time here was just tied to them on the previous page. It also brings up the Albright Manor and Jacqueline Holcroft, elements MIA since pages 5 and 6 respectively, but which seemed extremely important at the time, as well as bringing Cresh Glen back into things. I strongly suspect the twins are somehow at the center of this mystery. As for why she's a doll and why she turns D-002 into a doll? *Shrugs.* No idea. I wanna be turned into a doll, though.

On 1 Candle, given the year and the descriptions suggesting something violent and inhuman was at work here, the Abdi Residence incident feels connected with the creature that was connected with D-856 acquiring the mirror back in 3 candles. The Judith Montegru Memorial Library entry is interesting, with every number except "1" redacted in the date, and the strange line "Something small and defenseless originating from Jaqueline Holcroft," along with some other strange pieces, but I have no idea what to make of it. The Taichi Kazuo one is… REALLY interesting. It clearly depicts the aftermath of a game of Hyakumonogatari Kaidankai, with a mish-mash of JHorror references and Japanese ghostly cliches. Long black hair is strongly associated with ghosts in Japan and *especially* the Onryou of modern JHorror, water damage a la Dark Water, what seem to be cremains (both a Japanese death thing and a callback to earlier elements) in the water. It's delightful for someone like me, but it's hard to tell how much of it is relevant to the mystery. The repeated lines about candles, for sure, including the crossing out of living, and most likely "The sound it makes when we burn each witness in the crematorium."

0 Candles… I… does this seriously end with a screamer? I muted it reflexively because of autistic sound/startle sensitivity, but I'm still half-worried I missed something.

Hide these ramblings!

EDIT: Also, lesson learned: The preview button is actually super important, and I need to use it.

EDIT 2: And I found out all this analysis was pointless, because someone confused abusive puzzle design/mystery writing practices with cleverness. There's an early XKCD that's somewhat applicable to my attitude to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand what I just read, but I felt it in my guts. Absolutely outstanding work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sublime SCP. I'm glad I went looking for past SCPs on Halloween!

I'm also trying to put the pieces together, and it looks as though as of yet no solution fully confirmed, though Kirby's solutions have 99.9% certification. A couple of posts back someone posted a summary.
To add to this, I don't believe these were supposed to be standalone stories. Outside of the teletype, the articles keep referencing each other. Perhaps this is bait for readers to keep reading. If that's the case, I echo the last comment about it feeling kind of unsatisfying. It would be great if there were a logical way these all fit together, though with the anachronistic video game in WWII trenches, that's probably too fantastical.
I still think this is the case, because if it weren't - if these really were just stories told to keep the user reading - I think would've been a whole lot more tasteful for the author to explicitly indicate it at the end, instead of saying "This is meant to be solved." for us to just realize the stories were meaningless.

item.title: So is it a massive waste of time trying to connect things together?
item.content:encoded:
After spending bloody ages trying to figure this out and drawing some conclusions about various things but ultimately coming up clutching at straws… from reading theories on here it turns out it's just a bunch of unrelated (or related in various ways on a surface level) spooky stories that ultimately kills the reader?

Is there a more complex explanation for what's going on here or not?

I really enjoyed various parts of this, especially that exploration log, but the solution (if I'm understanding it correctly) just feels incredibly dissatisfying after the many hours I spent reading and re-reading the mystery trying to figure it out. I'm left immensely annoyed and not spooked, which is a shame.

I feel like I'm probably missing something(s?) major. I feel like I understand the what of what is happening here but not the how. Going to keep searching for explanations a little while longer because I'd love to upvote something that has clearly had so much put into it. Please stop me if that way lies madness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is very informative, and understandable in a clear way, and I love how you turned the page into some document that is interactable. I put a +1 for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This took me more than 45 minutes to read. My eyes hurt.

That said, this SCP was really fun and really spoopy.
Definitely at +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
preeeeetty creeeeeeeepy, even if the guy at the end looks a bit too much like a Yautja.

all joking aside, even though i haven't solved this article's mystery, i already absolutely love it. what really sold it for me was all the subtle stuff, like each page referencing the number of lit candles in subtle ways, the sudden mid-sentence shift to referring to D-002 as "it," and the unexplained copy of a late 80s video game found in 1914. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I met a link to this SCP saying it is the kill agent from swans proposal.
So this SCP was meant to be the attack from lower narrative to ours. The attempt of foundation to get rid of the constant changes to reality. Which detected in swans proposal.

I might be bit burned through reading that, so I might till miss several things before the work as a whole click together.
Either that or the kill agent started working.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for the effort and formatting alone, even if I don't understand a lick of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is creepy as shit and a fantastic read +1

item.title: SCP-5999 Ending Explained (Mostly)
item.content:encoded:
Overall, this SCP has been a trip. I've really enjoyed it the first time through and reading all of the comments, but in the weeks since I first read this, I really haven't stopped thinking it because I didn't feel like I 'solved' it. While I feel I had all of the pieces, I didn't feel like they fit together in a cohesive manner. So in this post, I'm going to do my best to explain the true 'ending' (as I see it) for anyone else who has been thinking about it. Minus one big question….

+ show block
– hide block

So, three other posts have already gone into a LOT of detail on what's going on here, so instead of rehashing all of their points, I’m just going to link to the real sleuths here and use that as the basis of my analysis:

  • Captain Kirby Comment #1
  • Captain Kirby Comment #2
  • Modulus for Consciousness

Captain Kirby basically gets it right (and adds a lot of analysis that I'm going to not mention), but there's .01% that's not quite correct. So here's the concise description of what (I believe) is going on here:

  • This is a sequel to Swann's Proposal. In that proposal, SCP-001 are the writers of the SCP Foundation website, aka everyone who has ever contributed directly to 'the database'. SCP-5999 is then a result of the foundation realizing it is being controlled by a bunch of horror writers. The document admits there's almost no way to contain SCP-001, except for one possible effort…
  • Protocol ZK-001-ALPHA, which is the untested (at the time of SCP-001) protocol to be able to neutralize SCP-001 through memetic agents. This would be done if the foundation felt that the horror writers were going to destroy the foundation and needed some way to neutralize it, even if it meant sacrificing their own world. Hold on to that thought for a bit.
  • Each section in SCP-001 are almost exclusively written by foundation members that are known for meta-fiction, with section 7 being written by Swann himself. They know that this is going to be read by us (SCP-001).
  • The candles at the top of the screen and the stories are a reference to 'A Gathering of One Hundred Supernatural Tales', a parlor game where folks light 100 candles and then tell each other scary stories. After each story is told, a person blows out a candles. After the final story is told and the final candle is blown out, the environment would 'create a safe haven for the vocation of spirits'. We get seven candles, and after each section, counting down from '7', one candle gets blown out. After story 1, a spirit gets released. The references to the chains give more support to this, with the chains getting weaker after each section and each candle being extinguished.
  • Sections 7 - 1 actually have nothing to do with SCP-5999. They're fun, engaging, and heavily trope reliant spooky stories, but they're not describing an entity. They're just stories to get you to continue moving forward. As a matter of fact, the only time SCP-5999 is ever really referenced is in section 0, when he is 'released'. No actual description of SCP-5999 is ever given because it's not important. Getting the reader to the end is what's important.

SCP-5999 is very different from almost every other SCP because almost every other SCP is describing how to contain an entity. This one is actually designed to release an entity. SCP-5999 is the thing you see in section 0, and the rest of the SCP are protocol ZK-001-ALPHA from Swann's proposal. You, the reader, are being neutralized by the foundation because you've been deemed a threat.

Now, as I wrote this, one question has been bugging me… ZK-001-ALPHA is incredibly dangerous. They know this could end their existence forever if this goes wrong. Even if they have to deal with terrible entities and horrifying situations, it's not worth risking their world. For SCP-5999 to be written, something must have been added to the database that is so horrendous that the risk of a ZK-Class Reality Failure scenario was worth it.

So what SCP made that risk worth it?

This was the SCP that finally made me create an account, start upvoting content, and really engage with the community. Thank you very much, Shaggy and the writing team for one of my new favorite SCPs.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This made me so disturbed I genuinely thought I was going to vomit from the tension. I wish I could upvote this a thousand times.

Ho-ly shit.

item.title: One of the best yet
item.content:encoded:
A fantastic utilization of the expanded scp site capabilities

i adored every page and the animation and the sounds

10/10 deserves more up doots

item.title: That was fun.
item.content:encoded: I have no idea what I just read, but I read all of it and I absolutely loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the atmosphere of this article, I really really do. But I can't help but agree with what a great many people said about it being confusing af (I know, it was designed to be like that, but still…).

Also, just a small detail that I found:

[…] tripping over something embedded in the gound […]

That being said, I was really engaged reading this. The feeling of something sinister going on is ever present, and that alone had me entertained all the way. Also the effort of the people involved in this is absolutely insane. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey i'm still sort of obsessed with figuring this out completely, are any more cookies going to be given out?

Still one of my fav SCPs, everyone who worked on it should be so proud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's diffy-q in this article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only thing harder than trying to understand this SCP is trying to understand why it has so many upvotes. When I read an SCP article I don't want to pull out a conspiracy billboard and solve differential calculus equations just to start to understand what it MIGHT be about. Easy downvote.

When I read and rated this a few days ago, it happened to coincide with rating +776, with mine as 777. Since it seemed too creepy to be true, today I revisited to check if people were trying to balance the rating at 777, but alas. Thank you, to all who contributed to this scip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Look man, cool concept, but it really is way to drawn out for the pay off. I couldn't even make it through the article the first time until a friend of mine told me the twist, and then it was a slog to get through. It was just to much of an edge fest, which I get is kinda the point, and the reference to the candles game and the general themes were cool

That being said, you clearly put a lot of time into this so good on you. The article didn't land for me but I like the concept, and judging by the rating it landed for a lot of people. Just because a passion project doesn't land for everyone, doesn't mean it shouldn't be pursued

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay. Well, you could be upset about that, or you could appreciate the entertainment until that point for what it was :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had originally given this an upvote on effort alone, even though I couldn't make heads or tails of it, and there's still a lot to like and admire about it, but after learning the actual explanation for all of it, I'm afraid I had to reverse my vote.

+spoiler
-

The reveal that it's (apparently) all just a meta shaggy dog story just kills it for me. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 just a heads up some of the things overlap with each other a tad: https://sta.sh/0i9j79w08y8 not sure if this is intentional but I figured you might like to know :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know what to think about it. I seems to be cool and mystery, but for my small head this article is too much of a mess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe author made it as a html. file(s). That way, page source should just contain the link to said file(s)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am i in danger or is this an inside joke im just not getting?

item.title: Re: MAJOR update
item.content:encoded: That blows, I genuinely liked the video at the end. Provided a nice punch.

item.title: Small mistake
item.content:encoded:
Excellent read, I'm not sure how it all links to eac other exactly, but I'll figure it out later. +1 for me.

Just wanted to note that at the end of the '1' section it says "Recovered Evidence provided by Containment Staff - SCP-5000" shoudn't that be changed to 5999?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: E: Ugh I just can't let anything stand in the way of other peoples' enjoyment, however tangentially or indirect. This is really good and you (reader) should just let it stand on its own and approach it as though it was a series of short stories you read in an obscure old anthology, for which there are no SparkNotes. I wish I had.

item.title: Re: MAJOR update
item.content:encoded:
It's still up on its original host

http://kaktuskontainer.wdfiles.com/local--files/7/freedom.mp4

item.title: Re: MAJOR update
item.content:encoded: If you're able to view the regular format it's unchanged. But as for the reduced format video, I'm not sure if it's saved anywhere.

item.title: Re: MAJOR update
item.content:encoded:
This is my first time reading the article and I was extremely confused why would it all be leading up to that. I thought "what? was that supposed to be scary? Is this a prank? Why does it have boobs?"

Anyway, is there a link to the actual video?

item.title: Re: MAJOR update
item.content:encoded:
ngl I thought that this was vandalised.

I guess it kinda was but damn. People gotta respect the work that went into these. C'mon people!

item.title: MAJOR update
item.content:encoded:
Due to multiple persons having issues with the final video appended to this entry, one that our friend VolgunStrife worked SO GODDAMN HARD ON to produce, I have made the decision to remove the offending content.

If you turn your attention to the "Reduced Format" version of this file, you will see the STELLAR replacement contributed by the esteemed Swaghetti.

You're welcome. I hope this is fucking scary enough for you now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for creeping me out enough that I noped right out. I’ll circle back around later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry that this one gets a bit long.

I genuinely loved the buildup of the article, and every disconnected (yet vaguely connected) addendum only heightened the creep factor and interest for me (which I'm guessing was the point). It was really interesting to see how these separate pieces seemed to just come together, and the horror of the whole thing just kept ramping up over time in that awesome way only Shaggy really pulls off. Credit where its due, the writing was awesome and the buildup was pretty great too. The jumpscare at the end hit the peak of my fear, and it was a good complement to all of the other crazy shit that got thrown at us. The containment protocols, description, timeline, and recovered materials really gave me the chills despite how cliche they were. It was also really cool how the candles were used and how by the end they were hinted to basically be a Lite version of the One Hundred Supernatural Tales game. The reference of the game at the very end was really sneaky too, and a well-placed clue.

+ That being said, I was really disappointed by the ending.
- That being said, I was really disappointed by the ending.

I don't really have a problem with pataphysics and that whole meta aspect of the Foundation - its another part of the wiki's culture and its been around since the 001 proposal (which also reminds me, hella clever to hide the 001 reference in the article's editing history - it felt like reading something you shouldn't have been able to see).

The thing is, I personally didn't like how it was all basically a ploy, with all of it being fake just to let the Foundation kill the readers. I felt like all of that storytelling just went out the window completely, with the anomalies really just being disconnected enough to get the reader to finish the article. The whole One Hundred Tales integration was perfectly done, and especially smart regarding how every part of the file is different, but that isn't really enough for me to justify me reading this.

All of the cooler minor themes, like what's going on in the Albright Manor, Prodigy, Priest, King's relation to the whole thing, the possession, the exploration log - all of it is just meaningless in the end, which is kind of my main problem. The Foundation lured everyone in with a ghost story to kill us, but now the story is overridden by its intent, making it feel meaningless. To me, it felt a little like a cop-out, since I was expecting some grandiose conspiracy about Holcroft being murdered inside the haunted Manor, etc. etc., something that'd tie everything together, even if it'd be loose connections. I was really invested to find out the whole meaning behind the phrase "This is where I died," (Like who is dead, why are they causing all of these things, how many are gone??) but when the story revealed that it was the reader who was supposed to die… well… I guess my candle snuffed out.

All of this being said, its not my cup of tea but it definitely is everyone elses! Also still giving a +1 for the awesome writing. May not have been satisfied by the overall ending/meaning, but again, that buildup/lure was stellar.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
As the sources for the two images in the Testing Log redirect to the images themselves, I will be providing the proper page links here:

First image is sourced to here, by Kendra Goering, under CC BY 2.0.

Second image is sourced to here, by foam, under CC BY-SA 2.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, I decided to read this initially because of the cool format, but continued reading because the narrative was extremely interesting. Was incredibly well written with compelling mystery so I applaud the writers. Still too bad it didn't win the 5000 competition yet this was definitely a worthy competition.

Still was very confused at the end of what the entire thing meant and it wasn't until I watched the video by The Exploring Series that it made total sense to be. That's okay though as I suppose that's the purpose. Good job to everyone else who was able to figure it out alone xD.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I very much enjoyed this, so I upvoted, which was when I realised the rating ticked up from 665 to 666.

"Very clever" thought I "How did you do that?".

I don't think you did though. Sooo that was a nice extra layer of creepy for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you open this page without the browser cache, the face image will not be mosaic. It's probably because the process starts before something is loaded, but I don't know the details.

DOMException: Failed to execute 'getImageData' on 'CanvasRenderingContext2D': The source width is 0.
at PixelFlow.setUpCanvas (http://scptestwiki.wdfiles.com/local--files/sigma%3Aseven/pixel-flow.cjs.js:218:32)
at new PixelFlow (http://scptestwiki.wdfiles.com/local--files/sigma%3Aseven/pixel-flow.cjs.js:35:10)
at HTMLDocument.<anonymous> (http://scptestwiki.wdfiles.com/local--files/sigma%3Aseven/main.js:178:26)
at e (https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/jquery/3.4.1/jquery.min.js:2:29453)
at t (https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/jquery/3.4.1/jquery.min.js:2:29755)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i'm super super bad at mysteries, but is there any significance to (spoilers?)

+ show block
– hide block

the repeated references to siblings (specifically twins)?

just something i noticed that i hadn't seen anyone talk about yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, heck. I think it's a pretty clear link.

We're assembling the anti-narrative, that much is clear, but why, and what is affected?

I'm going to also assume the false beast and the end guy are the antagonist mentioned.

Well, I'm also set that it's about us dying to the anomaly. The death of the horror author and podcaster give a clue to that.

Perhaps this is the experience of dying to a kill agent, as you form these narratives in your head and it kills your autobiographical-narrative. Probably not.

Am I close?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Magnum opus of S.D, in my opinion, would deserve the first place on the list.

Huge +

item.title: Michael or Micheal?
item.content:encoded: Is the character's name meant to be Michael but misspelled, or is it actually MEANT to be Micheal?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this was a relatively good scp. 8/10

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If anyone wants answers, try checking the articles edit history.

It might help.

(Yes, I looked it up, I'm not clever.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, whatever. I'll let folks draw their own conclusions from that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Specifically, most of the articles don't have that much cohesion

Horror anthologies — and anthologies in general — usually don't. They're stand-alone stories, for the most part.

all of them feel a little like they came from separate SCPs

Because they do — or, fictional write-ups of such.

It all feels like spooky settings for the sake of spooky settings.

Horror stories are set in conventional horror settings: more at 11

+ show block
– hide block

 I thought we banned video game SCPs for a reason for pete's sake.

This is a thing that has never even come remotely close to happening in this or any other universe, and is a complete fabrication, for pete's sake.

I thought that we didn't like deaths here

'We'? People like whatever it is they like.

they seemed cheap and pointless

Regarding the Exploration? Fair. But tropes are tools, and one would be a fool to disregard a tool when in cases where it serves a specific purpose.

the best writers on the friggin' site

Flattering, but we're far from it.

resorting to all-kill exploration logs, video game SCPs, and random disembowelments

Again, tropes are tools. I'd love to see more video-game creepypasta that can be fresh, personally. And if you think that last bit is 'random', then you're not paying close enough attention to what's happening in that section.

For that matter, why the heck did they send two siblings on an exploration together?

Maybe it's reality being unrealistic, but family members are able to serve together on the same unit (at least in the army, from a quick google search).


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: some little details/easter eggs I noticed
item.content:encoded:
collapsible just in case

+ show block
– hide block


1. OK so the first thing that i noticed is in part 2, "You complete me" I think that this is meant to reference a few things. The first is obviously the scp foundation trying to "complete" the remains of Holocroft, but i also think it refers to the sleep paralysis nightmare that a few effected people are having, i think that these nightmares are supposed to represent some force trying to make Holocraft "complete" again, thus "you" as the people who had the nightmares, are slowly completing Holocraft
2. the next one I found was during part three, "when the man comes around", this one is actually sorta important to how we think of this whole part. so right at the start we see a car playing a johnny cash song with some bible verse in it, so i looked it up, and theirs actually a johnny cash song called
"when the man comes around" i listened to the song and it's mostly about what happens when god (the man) comes down. So, now we probably know who that false beast might be as now we know that the "man" who came around to this town is, its an untrue god of some sort.
aaaand that is about all I've figured out so far that i haven't seen anyone else talk about, and id rather not get cocky and try to explain the whole darn thing when that's been so elegantly done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is an experience.

+1

+ A continuity error left from the 5k contest?
- 5000 != 5999

Recovered Evidence provided by Containment Staff - SCP-5000

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay so, considering an upvote, but… I do have a problem or two. Specifically, most of the articles don't have that much cohesion - other than the spooky chains and the "this is where I died" bit, as well as some other, admittedly clever bits of recursion, all of them feel a little like they came from separate SCPs. It all feels like spooky settings for the sake of spooky settings. I thought we banned video game SCPs for a reason for pete's sake. Admittedly, this may be just me "not getting it", and I know it's a criminal sin to not "get" SCPs, but at the same time, well… I never was good at puzzles, or reading between the lines.

Also, a couple nitpicks - too many deaths. I thought that we didn't like deaths here, that they seemed cheap and pointless. I don't mind them myself, I just thought that the best writers on the friggin' site would do better than resorting to all-kill exploration logs, video game SCPs, and random disembowelments. For that matter, why the heck did they send two siblings on an exploration together? Has the foundation ever heard of 'emotionally compromised'?!

Look, I'm going to go watch the "explained" video on this to figure out what the heck was going on, and reserve my judgement for then. I'll be back in a while. I'll say this - phenomenal writing, a real sense of dread, and major kudos for not having any 'proper' jumpscares.

EDIT: …oh…. ohhhhhhhh…. so it's related to… oh, I like it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You ask and you shall receive (the video, about a month later).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gonna have to give this a no. It is well made, I suppose but it just didn't hit for me at all. The end thing was just…medicore I suppose? Maybe some of the religious horror just fell utterly flat since I don't believe in that stuff.

I'll give credit where it's due even if I didn't enjoy it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From the old or new testament?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are specifics names for the fluids in your eyes: aqueous and vitreous humors.
